questions.q: State the basic rule for the force between electric charges.
options: Like charges attract; unlike charges repel
Like charges repel; unlike charges attract
All charges attract each other
All charges repel each other
questions.explanation: Like charges (two positives or two negatives) repel, while unlike charges (a positive and a negative) attract. This mirrors the rule for magnetic poles. Charging an object means transferring electrons — gaining them makes it negative, losing them makes it positive.

questions.q: Define what it means to say charge is quantised.
options: Charge can take any continuous value
Charge comes only in whole-number multiples of the elementary charge (≈1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C)
Charge can be created and destroyed
Charge is always conserved in a reaction
questions.explanation: Quantisation means charge exists only in whole-number multiples of the elementary charge (the charge on one electron or proton, ≈1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C) — you cannot have half an elementary charge. This is distinct from conservation (charge can only be transferred, never created or destroyed).

questions.q: A positively charged rod charges a neutral metal sphere by conduction (contact). Determine the sphere's final charge.
options: Negative (opposite sign)
Positive (same sign)
Neutral
It depends on the sphere's size
questions.explanation: In charging by conduction the charged object touches the conductor and charge is shared on contact, so the sphere ends up with the same sign as the rod — positive. (By induction, with no contact, it would end up with the opposite sign.) Mixing up conduction and induction is the most common electrostatics error.

questions.q: Compare the final charge produced by charging by conduction versus charging by induction.
options: Both give the same sign as the charging object
Conduction gives the same sign (by contact); induction gives the opposite sign (no contact)
Both give the opposite sign
Conduction gives the opposite sign; induction gives the same sign
questions.explanation: Conduction requires direct contact and leaves the object with the SAME sign as the charging object. Induction works without contact — the charged object separates the conductor's charges, which is then earthed and the object removed — leaving the OPPOSITE sign. This contrast (same/contact vs opposite/no-contact) is a classic exam discriminator.

questions.q: Two point charges experience a certain force. Predict the new force if one charge is doubled and the distance between them is also doubled.
options: The force is unchanged
The force becomes half
The force doubles
The force quarters
questions.explanation: By Coulomb's law F ∝ q₁q₂/r². Doubling one charge multiplies the force by 2; doubling the distance divides it by 2² = 4. Combined: 2 × ¼ = ½, so the force becomes half. The two changes don't cancel because charge is a direct factor while distance is squared.

questions.q: Define electric field strength and give its equation.
options: The total charge at a point; E = q/F
The force per unit positive charge at a point; E = F/q
The energy stored at a point; E = qV
The charge times distance; E = qr
questions.explanation: Electric field strength is the force per unit positive charge experienced at a point: E = F/q, in newtons per coulomb. The field is a property of the location (it exists with or without a charge there); the force F = Eq is what a specific charge feels. Confusing the field with the force is a common error.

questions.q: Identify the correct rules for drawing electric field lines.
options: Lines go from negative to positive and may cross
Lines go out of positive and into negative charges, never cross, and are closer where the field is stronger
Lines always form closed loops around a single charge
Lines point toward positive charges
questions.explanation: Field lines start on positive charges and end on negative charges (out of +, into −), never cross (the field has one direction per point), and are closer together where the field is stronger. Around a single positive charge they radiate outward; around a negative charge they point inward.

questions.q: Two parallel plates are 0.02 m apart with 100 V between them. Calculate the electric field strength between them.
options: 2 V m⁻¹
50 V m⁻¹
questions.explanation: For parallel plates the field is uniform: E = V/d = 100/0.02 = 5000 V m⁻¹ (equivalently N C⁻¹). It has that same value everywhere between the plates. This uniform-field setup is exactly how charged particles are accelerated and deflected in Module 6.

questions.q: Distinguish electric field strength from electric force.
options: They are the same quantity
Field strength (E = F/q) is a property of the location; force (F = Eq) is what a specific charge experiences in it
Force is a property of the location; field is what a charge feels
Field strength is measured in newtons; force in newtons per coulomb
questions.explanation: The electric field strength E = F/q is the force per unit charge — a property of the location created by other charges, existing whether or not a charge sits there. The electric force F = Eq is the actual push or pull on a specific charge q placed in the field. The field describes the location; the force describes what happens to a particular charge.

questions.q: A 3×10⁻⁶ C charge is placed in a uniform field of 4000 N C⁻¹ between parallel plates. Calculate the force on it.
questions.explanation: Using F = Eq = 4000 × 3×10⁻⁶ = 1.2×10⁻² N = 0.012 N. The force on a positive charge points in the field direction (from the positive plate toward the negative plate). Dividing instead of multiplying (giving 1.33×10⁻⁹) is the trap.
